description: Archived discoveries capture validated learning that shaped the current platform-engineering roadmap and internal product choices.

riskFramework.question: Will internal teams adopt and keep using the platform path?
Will the platform create enough leverage and risk reduction to justify continued investment?
Can teams understand and complete platform workflows without bespoke support?
Can the platform teams build and operate this reliably within current constraints?

items.title: Platform onboarding friction: validated discovery
items.name: Platform onboarding friction: validated discovery
items.summary: Discovery confirmed that mainstream product teams were willing to standardize, but onboarding still depended too much on hidden platform knowledge and manual support.
items.opportunity: Clarify whether a better portal, clearer templates, and measurable onboarding telemetry would move the platform from optional to preferred for both engineering managers and ordinary software engineers.

linkedInitiatives.description: Golden path adoption initiative focused on portal, template, and onboarding telemetry improvements.

assignments.how: Led customer interviews, template analysis, and the recommendation to treat onboarding as a first-class product workflow.
Validated discoverability and documentation issues in the current portal experience.
Provided direct evidence from onboarding support sessions and migration work.
Measured where the onboarding flow stalled and where repeated support work was concentrated.

riskFocus.question: Will internal teams adopt and keep using the platform path?
riskFocus.hypothesis: Platform adoption rises when onboarding becomes the easiest path rather than the most governed path.
evidenceToCollect: Whether ordinary software engineers can complete common onboarding tasks without tribal knowledge.
What proportion of support effort is caused by poor discovery versus true platform gaps.
Whether teams return to bespoke local tooling after onboarding.

items.title: Trusted supply chain baseline: validated discovery
items.name: Trusted supply chain baseline: validated discovery
items.summary: Discovery confirmed that teams accepted stronger supply-chain controls when those controls were embedded in templates, pipelines, and base artifacts rather than introduced as separate review work.
items.opportunity: Clarify whether stronger artifact provenance and trusted base images could be deployed as defaults without undermining team autonomy.

linkedInitiatives.description: Platform controls by default initiative focused on identity, policy, artifact, observability, and reliability workflows.

assignments.how: Led the control-baseline analysis and recommendation on which artifact and identity standards should be defaulted first.
Validated where delivery workflows could absorb stronger supply-chain controls without excessive friction.
Validated how trusted artifacts should appear in supported starting points and migration guidance.
Validated the operational value of stronger provenance and rollback trust during incidents.

riskFocus.question: Will the platform create enough leverage and risk reduction to justify continued investment?
riskFocus.hypothesis: Trusted artifacts are viable when they reduce platform-wide risk and incident costs more than they add friction to mainstream delivery.
evidenceToCollect: Which supply-chain checks ordinary software engineers experience as opaque blockers versus clear workflow steps.
How often teams hit unsupported artifact and dependency paths.
How much release risk is tied to weak provenance or outdated base images.
